text: The hyperscopic video shows the liver and gallbladder as the patient is positioned supine. The surgical team adjusts the bed to the Trendelenburg position, tilting the patient to improve visibility of the pelvis. The uterus and both ovaries are visible, with the right ovary identified as having a teratoma, although it is not clearly visible at this moment.

The surgeon introduces additional laparoscopic trocars, ensuring placement away from the clearly visible inferior epigastric artery and vein to avoid injury. The same procedure is performed on the left side, with trocars inserted away from the inferior periosteic vessels.

The Da Vinci robotic system is docked, and the surgeon examines the right ovary, which is largely occupied by a teratoma. Using endoshears, the surgeon makes a superficial incision on the ovary, carefully detaching the teratoma capsule from the ovarian capsule without penetrating the teratoma. The capsule of the teratoma appears very thin during this delicate dissection.

The surgeon inadvertently enters the teratoma, revealing yellow, thick fluid. A suction irrigator is introduced to aspirate the fluid, aiming to reduce inflammation risk. The pelvis is repeatedly irrigated to remove any cyst content. The scissors, assisted by the Marilyn, detach the teratoma capsule from the ovary with ease.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to clear the pelvis of liquid content after the cyst is completely removed. The ovary is irrigated, and areas of bleeding are gently made hemostatic using Maryland forceps to minimize injury to the remaining ovarian capsule. Chromopertubation is performed, introducing dye into the uterus and observing its flow through both fallopian tubes, confirming their patency. The camera is repositioned to a different laparoscopic port, and an endopouch bag is introduced through the umbilicus to remove the intact teratoma capsule.
